structured_choices.data_source: Hyperspectral drone imagery from three growing seasons (approximately 10,000 images) paired with yield measurements from agricultural reports and local government statistics. Data spans multiple fields across regions and years. Temporal coverage: three complete growing seasons with consistent collection methodology.
structured_choices.label_definition: Crop yield is defined as the total harvested mass (or standardized yield per unit area) as recorded in agricultural reports and government statistics for each field-season combination. Yield values are continuous; outliers (>3 standard deviations from field-season mean) are flagged and retained with notation but not removed, as they may represent legitimate variation or data entry errors requiring domain review.
structured_choices.split_method: Spatial-temporal stratified split. Primary split: by growing season (Season 1 train, Season 2 validation, Season 3 test) to prevent temporal leakage and ensure the model generalizes to unseen years. Secondary stratification: within each season, fields are grouped by spatial proximity (using k-means clustering on field coordinates or a grid-based approach) to ensure train/val/test sets do not contain spatially adjacent fields. This prevents the model from learning spatial autocorrelation artifacts rather than true yield drivers. Split proportions: 60% train, 20% validation, 20% test (by field-season pairs, not individual images; multiple images per field-season are kept together to avoid within-field leakage).
structured_choices.preprocessing_fit_point: All preprocessing transformations are fit exclusively on the training set and applied identically to validation and test sets. Hyperspectral dimensionality reduction (e.g., PCA, band selection) is fit on training set only. Scaling (standardization) of raw hyperspectral bands is fit on training set statistics. Yield labels are standardized using training set mean and variance for regression stability, and inverse-transformed for final metric reporting in original yield units.
structured_choices.features: Primary features: dimensionality-reduced hyperspectral bands obtained via PCA (retaining 95% variance) or domain-guided band selection (indices like NDVI, GNDVI, and red-edge bands known to correlate with crop health). Secondary features: temporal metadata (day of year, growing degree days if available), field identifiers (treated as categorical to allow field-specific intercepts but not used as spatial coordinates). Features do not include yield data, future weather, or any information collected after the hyperspectral survey date. Feature importance analysis is performed post-hoc to verify that learned features are agronomically plausible.
structured_choices.model: Convolutional neural network (CNN) architecture: 2D convolutions applied to hyperspectral image patches, followed by global average pooling and 2–3 dense layers, outputting a single continuous yield prediction. Architecture details: 3–4 convolutional blocks (32–128 filters, 3×3 kernels, ReLU activation), batch normalization, dropout (0.3–0.5), and final regression head. Regularization: L2 weight decay and early stopping on validation loss. Training: Adam optimizer, initial learning rate tuned on validation set.
structured_choices.baseline: Multi-linear regression (MLR) using the same dimensionality-reduced hyperspectral features and temporal metadata as the CNN. The MLR baseline is tuned with equivalent effort: ridge and lasso regularization parameters are selected via cross-validation on the training set over a reasonable grid (alpha in [0.001, 1.0]). Both CNN and MLR receive identical feature engineering, scaling, and label standardization. This ensures that any performance difference is attributable to model capacity, not feature advantage. Secondary baseline: persistence model (predicting field average yield from Season 2) provides an operational lower bound.
structured_choices.primary_metric: Root Mean Squared Error (RMSE) in original yield units (e.g., kg/hectare or tons/acre). RMSE is chosen because: (1) it is interpretable to farmers (directly comparable to yield scale), (2) it penalizes large errors heavily, aligning with agronomic risk (a 10-ton prediction error is materially worse than a 1-ton error), and (3) it does not require threshold selection or class balancing adjustments (yield is continuous). RMSE is computed on the held-out test set and reported with 95% confidence intervals via bootstrap resampling (n=1000 replicates).
secondary_metrics: Mean Absolute Error (MAE) — interpretable average error magnitude in yield units
R² coefficient of determination — measures proportion of variance explained, contextualizing RMSE against field-level yield variability
Prediction interval calibration — whether predicted 90% intervals contain true yields at approximately 90% frequency, important for farmer confidence
Spatial residual autocorrelation (Moran's I) — tests whether errors are randomly distributed across space or exhibit clustering, indicating potential overfitting or unmodeled spatial confounds
structured_choices.model_selection_data: Hyperparameters for both CNN and MLR are selected using the validation set (Season 2) via grid search or random search. Validation metrics (RMSE, R²) guide architecture decisions (e.g., CNN depth, dropout rate) and regularization strength. No information from the test set (Season 3) is used during model selection. Early stopping for the CNN is implemented using a held-out subset (10%) of the training set, or equivalently, monitored on the full validation set.
structured_choices.test_set_policy: The test set (Season 3 data, spatially isolated fields) is used exactly once, at the end, to report final performance. No hyperparameter tuning, threshold selection, or model retraining occurs after test set evaluation. If multiple CNN architectures are proposed, the choice between them is made on the validation set beforehand; the single final model is then evaluated on the test set.
structured_choices.confound_controls: Weather and soil quality variations are acknowledged as unobserved confounds. The experiment does not claim to establish causation; it establishes predictive accuracy under realistic conditions. To partially mitigate confounding, the analysis includes: (1) stratification by field-season to ensure each field appears in train, val, and test across different years (if data permits), reducing unmeasured field-level heterogeneity; (2) post-hoc residual analysis to identify whether errors correlate with known regional or seasonal patterns, signaling unmodeled confounds; (3) ablation studies removing spectral bands to assess sensitivity to feature choice, indirectly testing robustness to confounding by unobserved variables. The experiment establishes that CNN predictions outperform MLR under the given observational conditions; it does not establish why (i.e., does not isolate crop physiology from confounding factors).

design_narrative:
## Experiment Design: CNN vs. MLR for Hyperspectral Crop Yield Prediction

### Objective
To test whether a convolutional neural network trained on hyperspectral drone imagery predicts crop yield more accurately than traditional multi-linear regression, using a properly controlled experimental design that respects temporal and spatial structure in the data.

### 1. Data and Labeling
The experiment uses hyperspectral imagery and corresponding yield measurements from three growing seasons, totaling approximately 10,000 images across multiple agricultural fields. Yield is defined as the total harvested mass (or mass per unit area) recorded in agricultural reports and government statistics for each field-season combination. Yield values are continuous; extreme outliers (>3 SD from seasonal field mean) are flagged for review but retained in analysis, as they may represent legitimate variation rather than measurement error.

### 2. Train-Validation-Test Split Strategy
A spatial-temporal stratified split is employed to prevent both temporal leakage and spatial overfitting:
- **Temporal stratification:** Season 1 data forms the training set, Season 2 forms the validation set, and Season 3 forms the held-out test set. This prevents future information from influencing model training and tests generalization to genuinely unseen years.
- **Spatial stratification:** Within each season, fields are grouped by geographical proximity using spatial clustering (k-means on field coordinates or grid-based assignment). Train, validation, and test sets do not share spatially adjacent fields, preventing the model from exploiting spatial autocorrelation rather than learning true yield drivers.
- **Unit of split:** The field-season is the unit of split (not individual images). All hyperspectral images from a given field in a given season remain together, assigned to the same fold, preventing leakage from multiple images of the same field-season pair into different sets.
- **Proportions:** Approximately 60% of field-season pairs to training, 20% to validation, and 20% to test.

### 3. Feature Engineering and Preprocessing
Hyperspectral bands (raw dimensionality: ~200+ bands) are reduced using principal component analysis (PCA) retaining 95% of variance, or via agronomically-informed band selection (e.g., NDVI, GNDVI, red-edge indices) known to correlate with crop health. PCA transformation is fit on the training set only and applied to validation and test sets using the training-set-derived components. All raw hyperspectral values are standardized (zero mean, unit variance) using training set statistics. Yield labels are standardized for numerical stability during model training and inverse-transformed for final metric reporting in original yield units (e.g., kg/hectare).

Categorical features (field ID, crop type) are included as fixed effects or embeddings to capture field-level heterogeneity without introducing spatial leakage. Temporal metadata (day of year, growing degree days) are included if available but are not future-dated relative to the hyperspectral survey.

### 4. Models and Baseline
**CNN model:** A 2D convolutional neural network with 3–4 convolutional blocks (32–128 filters, 3×3 kernels), ReLU activations, batch normalization, and dropout (rate 0.3–0.5). Convolutions operate on hyperspectral image patches; outputs are global-average-pooled and passed through 2–3 dense layers to a single regression head. Regularization: L2 weight decay (tuned on validation set) and early stopping. Optimizer: Adam with initial learning rate selected via validation-set search.

**MLR baseline:** Multi-linear regression using the same dimensionality-reduced hyperspectral features and temporal metadata as the CNN. Ridge (L2) and lasso (L1) regularization parameters are tuned via cross-validation on the training set over a grid (alpha ∈ [0.001, 1.0]). The MLR baseline receives identical feature engineering and scaling, ensuring that any performance difference is attributable to model expressiveness, not feature advantage.

**Secondary baseline:** A persistence model predicting each field's average yield from the validation season provides an operational lower bound (naive forecast).

### 5. Evaluation Metrics
**Primary metric:** Root Mean Squared Error (RMSE) in original yield units (e.g., kg/hectare). RMSE is chosen because it is agronomically interpretable, directly comparable to the scale of yield variation, and penalizes large prediction errors heavily—aligning with farmer priorities (a 10-ton error is materially worse than a 1-ton error). RMSE is computed on the held-out test set with 95% confidence intervals via bootstrap resampling (n=1000 replicates).

**Secondary metrics:** Mean Absolute Error (MAE), R² coefficient of determination (contextualizes RMSE against total field-level variance), prediction interval calibration (90% confidence intervals should contain true yields ~90% of the time), and spatial residual autocorrelation (Moran's I test) to detect residual clustering indicative of unmodeled spatial confounds or overfitting.

### 6. Model Selection and Validation Approach
Hyperparameter tuning for both CNN and MLR is performed using the validation set (Season 2). Grid or random search selects regularization parameters, CNN architecture depth, and dropout rates by minimizing validation-set RMSE. The test set (Season 3) is held out and used exactly once, at the end, to report final performance. No retraining, threshold adjustment, or model selection occurs after test-set evaluation.

### 7. Confound Control and Scope
Weather patterns and soil quality variations are acknowledged as unobserved confounds that influence crop yield but are not directly measured. The experiment does not establish causation; it establishes predictive accuracy under realistic field conditions. Mitigation strategies include: (1) spatial-temporal stratification reducing unmeasured field-level confounding, (2) post-hoc residual analysis to identify residual patterns correlated with region or season (signaling unmodeled confounds), and (3) sensitivity analysis (feature ablation) assessing robustness to feature engineering choices. The finding will be: CNN predicts yield more accurately than MLR on held-out test data under these observational conditions—not why this occurs or whether CNN has learned crop physiology versus capturing confounded patterns.
